item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, and the story you built around it. Admittedly, it felt a little slow sometimes, and I skimmed a bit, but the ending was well worth the read!

I'm sharing this with my cousin who likes birds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview was conducted through writing, so the robin physically wrote his responses. It seems obvious to me that if a bird wrote something, it would come out differently than if a human wrote it. This could be due to differences in intelligence, physical ability (I assume the bird was writing with his foot, doesn't sound easy), or just culture. Different dialects exist, and that doesn't make them wrong. Some people spell colour differently, some pronounce keter differently. Yeah, the Foundation COULD retroactively "fix" the bird's different spellings and grammatical choices, but they SHOULDN'T. Because if they did, it would change the reader's perception of the interviewee.

I gave this a solid +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There’s a crazy amount of spelling errors in the first interview log, starting after he says “the great flocktender”.

I don’t care that it’s intentional, do you think the foundation doesn’t have an editor. Definite -1 just for that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Yes, your new colleague is a seagull. The world is a weird place. Welcome to the Foundation." I cracked up at this. This was a really good skip.

+1

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Thank you for confirming!

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: I’m fine with Boro610’s edits, they PM’d me about them.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that I reverted the edit made since technically it's a stylistic edit, but reverted over that again after closer inspection of the text (which is admittedly a bit difficult to parse in the source window).

Author, can you weigh in here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I came here because I wanted to translate it… I upvoted it to 200, so congrats to the author!

EDIT: Is it First Kingdom of the Oak, or Kingdom of the First Oak? Both versions appear in the text and it doesn't look intentional.
EDIT2: Also improper code (lack of one ">") in the table hides one sentence in the last row after the stricken one.

The Perritree follows an extremely religious oligarchy with "Medila" at the highest.

item.title: not staff but...
item.content:encoded: the author stated here that the typos in the interview were intentional, so the typo "fixes" in revisions 25 and 26 were against author intention. the addition of "in" in revision 24 actually did improve the article, so i reverted to that one.
What can I say except, Team Bird Best Team!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like the final note, it has a good balance of verisimilitude and absurdity. Unfortunately the rest of the skip felt padded out, uninteresting, and with no real hook to keep me engaged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I like this in concept and execution, there are a lot of spelling issues that can be a bit distracting for me.

It is known that a village consisting of 14 pelicans exists in a single village Southern Africa…

This clause doesn’t make a whole lot of sense. Perhaps remove “a single village”? Also, I’d recommend changing “Southern Africa” to be a bit more specific (maybe a certain country?) - Africa is not a small place, after all.

Duffalan was an SCP-3095-1 instance acting as the official ambassador of the First Kindgom of the Oak

Should be “First Kingdom” instead of Kindgom.

The others continu to stay mindless. I wouldnot blame you for not attributeing your minds to her…

Are these mistakes intentional? I understand that a bird is writing this in English and so may make mistakes, but I’d recommend indicating that these are misspelled in-universe if so. If not, there is no “e” in “attributing”, “continu” should be continue, and “wouldnot” should be two words.

I have seen an apes…

Should be “I have seen apes” or “I have seen an ape”.

Our researchers have attributed several mindful theorys about how our world works.

The plural of theory is theories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the esoteric approach taken to the concept, but it's too long and feels padded out to simply expand on more details without being as interesting. The interview sort of lost my interest, but what really killed it for me was the incident. It upped the stakes without feeling like it was actually increasing the interest gained from it, and as a result, by the time the final note came around, I couldn't feel invested in the skip.

A minor complaint, but I think the numerous crosslinks feel like padding as well. While interesting tertiary information (at least, from the perspective as someone coming to this series from this article; I have no idea if it's relevant later), it doesn't feel like it helps the article much.
Not the one I thought would be my second skip to cross the +100 line, but in any case I’m glad my third skip managed to get to this number of upvotes. Thanks to everybody who read this one!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I’d rather not do the MTF crosslinks, if that’s fine with you. IMO, they clutter up the page. However, I am fine with the GOC crosslink.

As for the other skips, I like the choice of the skips, though not how they’re linked. If anything, I’d take the sentence:

These civilizations are often grouped by species.

And make a link out of that instead.

Also, this page is HTML, so if you want to link you need to use this syntax:

+ show block
– hide block
 <a target=“_top” href=“http://scp-wiki.net/scp-link”>text that you can click on to take you to a link</a>

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so this one has a lot of room for potential crosslinking, both in-line and otherwise. The mentions of MTFs like Nu-7 and Tau-5, as well as the mention of the GOC can be linked to their respective hubs. Additionally, the article is very reminiscent of SCP-1845 and SCP-2050, which can be denoted in text. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Civilizations created by SCP-3095-1 instances are to be monitored by MTF Eta-1 ("Birdbrains"). Should a civilization reach a point where it poses a threat to the Foundation and/or the veil, MTF Nu-7 ("Hammer Down") is authorized to destroy it.

To this:

Civilizations created by SCP-3095-1 instances are to be monitored by MTF Eta-1 ("Birdbrains"). Should a civilization reach a point where it poses a threat to the Foundation and/or the veil, MTF Nu-7 ("Hammer Down") is authorized to destroy it.

Changing this:

SCP-3095-1 were first discovered in New York City, after an organized group made up of pigeons attempted to claim the Statue of Liberty for themselves. They were quickly neutralized by Global Occult Coalition forces.

To this:

SCP-3095-1 were first discovered in New York City, after an organized group made up of pigeons attempted to claim the Statue of Liberty for themselves. They were quickly neutralized by Global Occult Coalition forces.

Changing this:

MTF Tau-5 ("Samsara") was deployed to the sites of Incident 3095-8, where they joined the forces of on-site personnel in order to resist the attack. [DATA REDACTED] authorized the use of SCP-████ in order to destroy The Perritree's major population center in Columbia. A large cave system was uncovered following the blast, where The Perritree had been developing advanced technology for approximately █ years. With this technology, The Perritree had secretly become a Level VI civilization.

To this:

MTF Tau-5 ("Samsara") was deployed to the sites of Incident 3095-8, where they joined the forces of on-site personnel in order to resist the attack. [DATA REDACTED] authorized the use of SCP-████ in order to destroy The Perritree's major population center in Columbia. A large cave system was uncovered following the blast, where The Perritree had been developing advanced technology for approximately █ years. With this technology, The Perritree had secretly become a Level VI civilization.

Changing this:

Although lone SCP-3095-1 instances have been regularly encountered, SCP-3095-1 instances tend to form civilizations. These civilizations are often grouped by species. For example, an SCP-3095-1 civilization may consist of robins, with very few other specimens present. The populations of these civilizations vary wildly. It is known that a village consisting of 14 pelicans exists in a single village Southern Africa, while the largest known civilization had a population estimated in the millions across multiple large cities.

To this:

Although lone SCP-3095-1 instances have been regularly encountered, SCP-3095-1 instances tend to form civilizations, similar to SCP-1845 and SCP-2050. These civilizations are often grouped by species. For example, an SCP-3095-1 civilization may consist of robins, with very few other specimens present. The populations of these civilizations vary wildly. It is known that a village consisting of 14 pelicans exists in a single village Southern Africa, while the largest known civilization had a population estimated in the millions across multiple large cities.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, your new colleague is a seagull. The world is a weird place. Welcome to the Foundation.

Instant +1. Amazing work here, though I am admittedly a sucker for civilization type skips.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
That would be my bad for adding the navigation bar, then.

Well, I suppose there's a bright side to this: SCP-3095 went up by >15 points!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Noting that I don't really want this to explicitly be a part of the Team Bird thing, so I reverted the edits. I have added a crosslink to it, however.

item.title: Team bird best team!
item.content:encoded: +1 for the smart talking birds! Praise Medila!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah bugger. Serves me right for not checking the discussion first. I guess Duffalan needs to go back to school for a little longer!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you have noscript on, I don't think it shows up

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is up with this, I can't see it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the record, those are intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure if it's intentional, but I saw a few typos in the first interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the twist here a lot, even if it is kind of silly.

I have to echo previous complaints about writing quality and the unnecessary code. The loading delay was kind of pointless, and I had to reload the page for the second version to open properly. I don't see why this couldn't have just been done with listpages(? I'm not great with code) a la SCP-2111.

Quality-wise, some of the sections are still a little hard to read and lacking in clinical tone, but mostly I noticed a ton of spelling errors and typos, especially in the interview and later in the first version. Salted bombs? Columbia/Columbian? (The nation is Colombia.) Just, please, give it another proofread.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
my reaction, after reading the first part: aw, the birdies :(
my reaction, after reading the revision: yay, good birdies!
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go ahead, I'd love to see this in a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My god, this is amazing.

… Can I use this in a tale? Please?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: indeed, yes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made up entirely out of Anas platyrhynchos, or Mallard. Centered around three distinct population centers at the perimeter of the ███████ Lake in North Dakota. Culture is centered around technological advancement for the purpose of ascending to "Medila". This is the first known civilization to have more than one major population center, and the first to have reached Type V civilization status. Due to the rapid technological advancement of this SCP-3095-1 civilization, SCP-3095 was reclassified as Keter.

Upvoting for ducks ascending to godhood

EDIT: I missed the classifications and assumed you were using the Kardishev scale. Still like it anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastically written and the idea is executed amazingly. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Worth it just for that final sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really wrote this in HTML because I know it better than markdown, TBH. Plus, the revision effect.

I'm sorry you didn't like it. I'm a bit busy right now, but I'll see if I can revise it at a later date.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the biggest weakness of this is the actual writing style, than anything we usually deal with on the wiki. The description section's sentences are extremely choppy, like each one was written at a different time (other than the obvious nature of our linear existence, ya furious pedants.)

It ends up being hard to read, and while I enjoy the concept, from a writing perspective, this needs work.

Although lone SCP-3095-1 instances have been regularly encountered, SCP-3095-1 instances tend to form civilizations. These civilizations are often grouped by species. For example, an SCP-3095-1 civilization may consist of robins, with very few other specimens present. The populations of these civilizations vary wildly. It is known that a village consisting of 14 pelicans exists in a single village Southern Africa, while the largest known civilization had a population estimated in the millions across multiple large cities.

This is a good example. Declarative statement. Declarative statement. Descriptive statement (good, adds to the flow.) Declarative statement. Declarative statement. Each of these declarations had nothing to do with each other and really detracted from the overall reading ease of the piece.

Downvote for writing quality, unfortunately.

Also, I'm not really sure why you wrote this as a raw website page, rather than using wikidot syntax. I didn't notice any particular need for the scripting, other than the delay in loading in, which I don't feel added much. The Double Collapsibles also weren't particularly necessary, especially for the effort this must have taken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it's about birbs +1
it's about sapient birbs +1
birbs +10000

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
that being 3095 actually describes a "phenomenon" which we never really learn more about.

IMO, I thought the phenomenon is birds gaining sapience, as said in:

Specimens affected by SCP-3095, designated SCP-3095-1, will acquire a certain degree of sapience.

But yes, the description can be made clearer and more explicit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would just like to say that this SCP confirms my suspicion that you are, in fact, a seagull. I asked you on the chat if you were a seagull, and you said no, but why would any non-seagull write an article that portrayed seagulls as inherently superior to other birds? Everyone knows corvids are the smartest birds, but you didn't even mention them, because you are a seagull with an intrinsic bias towards your own species.

You get an upvote, but I know you're a seagull, not_a_seagull.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
while there is still a pretty big flaw in my eye with the article (that being 3095 actually describes a "phenomenon" which we never really learn more about. Like it's tied to the rituals, so does that make it an effect of the rituals, the rituals themselves, or this bird God Medila?), several little bits here and there make up for that somewhat. Those being:
1."…affecting members of the Aves class, also known as birds." hilarious phrasing.
2. The mental image of a bird trying to mine for iron.
3. The mental images of pigeons trying to claim the statue of liberty
4. The cool formatting thing you did at the end.

So yeah, neutral vote leaning towards yes (although i'll probably guilt myself into a full on yes vote later, who knows?).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assume you were there when he talked about this being a social experiment…eh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hahahahah I like it because bird is like your name which has seagull in it not_a_seagull

in all honesty I actually like it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely enjoyed this a lot more than your previous skips. The image of a foundation site staffed by sapient Seagulls is just an amazing image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't say I'm a fan, tbh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of two SCPS that I liked.

  1. SCP 2337 if he was actually useful.
  2. I don't know which one. It was up for a day and got taken down. It was a chicken that became a doctor if somebody was sick and would give them a cure, then become a chicken again.

Those ideas seem like this was a combination of those two. We have seagulls working with Kain Pathos Crow. Look him up and picture that… AWESOME. Upvoted vehemently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will admit I have my reservations about this, and it turned out alright. Faking deaths are quite a messy affair, I would say.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thank you to Pendleton_Miller, Waterfire, Logan005, Shio and probably even more site19 denizens for critique. Thank you to taylor_itkin for helping majorly with my dialogue.

Edit: I'd like to bring attention to a chinese translation of this work by Cynthia7979.

Edit: I would also like to bring attention to a Spanish translation by berstager
